data.title: A Look Around Markets In A Scary Post-FOMC Morning - Market Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Yesterday's FOMC meeting marked the beginning of a rough session for traders around the globe.

Global stock indexes took large hits, correcting down 3% in Japan, London, and Europe and gapping down starkly in this morning's open.

Dow Jones Index 4H chart – Source: TradingView

As you can see on the index chart for the Dow Jones Industrial Index, heavy selling pressure from a massive hawkish repricing has applied large pressure on US equities and others around the world.

Yesterday's conference from Jerome Powell did not help to contain the initial scare of higher rates for longer – as a matter of fact, his wordings around the current 3.50%-3.75% were estimated to be located "at the plausible range of neutral [rates]", implying that further movement in the Fed policy would only be contingent on a weakening economy or labor market.

And we haven't seen any sign of this for now – quite the contrary: jobless claims came at 205K, the lowest since January (and they were at two-year lows at that time)

This also combines with a flurry of central bank decisions (including the ECB, Bank of England, Bank of Japan and the Swiss National Bank), which haven't communicated any type of dovishness.

When assuming that crude oil prices are now 40% higher than they were the last trading day of February and close to 90% higher since their January lows, it is difficult not to reprice some heavy form of inflation spike.

Everybody feels it at the pump; it hasn't been fun.

Market Reactions

Crude oil spikes higher but eases since, Brent-WTI spread widens even more

European and Asian markets are getting even more concerned by the latest escalation in energy infrastructure attacks across the Middle East, as these regions remain heavily affected by supply disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

A detailed crude oil analysis will be coming at the top of the hour.

US Treasuries volatility spikes, curve flattens

Bond traders are now largely undoing the steepening that was priced in the rate curve (implying that rates will be lower in the near term than the long term).

The issue with this is that such curve conditions tighten credit and largely hurt banks.

This is also the type of curve movement that can precede either rate hikes or recessions – two conditions that aren't the most favorable for stock markets or any other markets, as a matter of fact.

In general, a flatter yield curve means that current inflation is expected to be higher than long-term inflation – this comes with the heavy rises in WTI.

The US dollar marks a top after the FOMC

Markets may at least reconcile with the fact that the US dollar is now correcting back to its pre-FOMC levels – this happens at the top of its July 2025 range (from 96.00 to the current 100.00).

An in-depth US dollar analysis will be coming up this afternoon, but the general theme to keep in mind is the fact that an easing dollar may also ease general pressures around markets since the beginning of the war.

Watch the 4H 50-MA that has, however, just acted as support – bouncing here would reinforce the pre-open panic.

This pullback has helped the JPY to relief, and similarly for other currencies – the USD is now at the bottom of the FX board in today's session. The correction in WTI is also helping.

Gold retests its February lows

The price action in gold and other metals has been very ugly, as participants really price out the global rate cuts, giving relative strength to yielding assets.

This phenomenon is magnified by the lack of consistent rallies since the beginning of the war and a general scare of a long-term inflationary process.

Still, it's not the end of the world for metals until $4,400 breaks – here could be a decent, small DCA entry point for those who did not participate in the trend.

Below $4,400, however, may see further correction back to $4,000 and below, $3,500, so make sure to watch for your size and add only on confirmation!

Cryptos are tumbling

Bitcoin is now back below $70,000; Ethereum remains relatively strong at $2,100, but the overall market is getting hurt by the latest hawkish repricing.

Keep a close eye on headlines and flows throughout the day, as today is promised to be quite volatile.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold Price Retreats Toward $4,629 After Fed Hold And Oil Shock Tighten Macro Squeeze
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) fell again on Thursday, March 19, with spot bullion trading near $4,630 as traders reacted to a tougher rate backdrop a day after the Federal Reserve stayed on hold. The metal has not been able to turn the geopolitical stress into a durable bid, with rising energy costs and firmer rate expectations instead pulling money away from non-yielding assets.

Gold is now pressing against an area that matters. The slide toward $4,629 has left the market sitting near its weakest levels since early February, and the tone remains heavy after a run of steady daily losses.

The chart no longer looks like a pause inside an uptrend. It looks more like a market still shedding length, with rebounds staying brief and sellers showing up before price can rebuild above nearby resistance around $4,700.

Momentum also appears tired rather than washed out. If gold goes down from the $4,600 zone in a clear way, the next point of interest could emerge closer to $4,500. To slow the pressure, bulls likely need a recovery back through the $4,700-4,750 area first.

Safe haven logic runs into rate reality

Normally, a geopolitical shock of this size would hand gold a cleaner lift. This time the reaction has been more complicated because the same conflict pushing up demand for safety is also driving oil sharply higher and reviving inflation anxiety.

That has kept the rate story front and center. With the Fed holding policy steady at 3.50-3.75% and showing no clear rush to deliver easier settings, the opportunity cost of holding bullion has become harder to ignore.

The broader cross-asset picture has added to that pressure. The prices for energy have jumped all over the world. Global risk appetite has weakened, and markets have started to recalibrate around a world where central banks may need to stay restrictive for longer than many had hoped only a few weeks ago.

What could shift next

A stabilization scenario is still on the table. If oil gives back part of its latest surge and Treasury pricing begins to reflect a less-threatening inflation pulse, gold could find room to repair and work back toward $4,700, with a stronger recovery opening the way to the $4,800 area.

The opposite path is easier to see for now. If energy prices remain elevated and traders keep pushing out the timing of policy easing, gold may remain under pressure, with a break below $4,600 exposing a deeper move toward $4,500.

The current gold market feels like a standoff. While geopolitical tensions keep people from selling gold entirely, the high cost of holding it in a high-rate environment is preventing any serious rallies from taking hold.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: PLDT: Defensive With Its Solid Fundamentals And Continued Data Center Investment
data.description:
In the months that followed my last coverage of PLDT, Inc. (PHI), we saw how it regained its strength and proved it deserved to be the other half of the telco duopoly along with Globe. My concerns about the increasing competitiveness of Converge made sense in all honesty. However, I also didn’t realize that PLDT is already a well-established and well-equipped telco giant with vast network coverage and continued innovation, including digital finance. Today, the Philippine stock market is being tested once again as panic rises amid higher oil prices and potential stagflation. Despite this, I believe that PHI is still an ideal defensive player in my portfolio.

PLDT 2025: A Solid Yearend

In recent years, the Philippine or PH government has increased its efforts to make the telco sector more competitive by accepting newcomers like Converge and DITO Telecom. This was aimed at protecting consumer interests and promoting fairness in the industry. After all, it has been operating and dominating the industry for a long time along with Globe Telecom. Even so, PLDT remains solid, supported by the continued digital transformation and data center expansion. We can see it in its most recent performance.

For the whole year, its operating revenue amounted to PHP 218.4B, up by 0.7% YoY from PHP 216.8B. This shows sustained growth amid macroeconomic volatility in the country and increased competition. Of course, one cannot simply ignore the slowing revenue growth in recent quarters. In fact, its Q4 revenues weakened by 1.4% YoY, unlike in the first three quarters of 2025, which reported positive growth. If you look at its average revenue per unit or ARPU, you can see the notable decrease in its Fiber segment. The consolation is that the mobile segment remained stable, particularly its Smart Prepaid.

On a lighter note, PLDT maintains a solid subscriber base. Its mobile subscribers increased by 901,713 subscribers YoY to a total of 59,891,520. More interestingly, postpaid subscribers have increased uninterruptedly in the past five quarters. The same applies to broadband subscribers, which also increased consistently for five consecutive quarters. All in all, PLDT had a net increase of over 1.2M subscribers in only a year. They completely offset the weakness in its fixed line segment.

Likewise, the operating costs and expenses rose. But if you look at it carefully, it was depreciation and amortization expenses that really drove the increase. In fact, the actual operating costs, especially the general operating costs and cost of devices, decreased. This shows well-managed cost-sensitivity amid increasing prices. In the end, the operating margin decreased but remained high at 23.0%, which means that its revenues remained high enough to cover all operating expenses. Additionally, the EBITDA margin reached 50.3% versus 49.2% YoY.

Challenges

Inflation remains a challenge even for telco companies like PLDT. After settling at 0.9%, it regained its momentum and rose again to its 13-month high of 2.4%, showing its stubbornness. In addition, the unemployment rate rebounded to 5.8% last January. This has been its highest level since June 2022. Considering this (supported by my first-hand experience), the PH economy is still a bit far from complete recovery. This may be worsened by the political climate and corruption issues here. The rift between our president and vice president may intimidate potential investments and even lead to capital flight. The combination of all these things may affect the purchasing or subscribing power of PLDT customers. This may weaken mobile data usage growth or discourage broadband upgrades. Cost pressures may rise as well, which may squeeze its margins and pricing flexibility.

Another problem it must watch out for is the skyrocketing oil prices. Currently, they are spiking toward $90-100 per barrel due to potential supply shocks in the Middle East. Additionally, fuel here has already had double-digit price hikes in only two weeks. With that, the PH market is currently experiencing higher energy costs and even trade disruptions. This can exacerbate inflation, which may affect PLDT’s costs this year. Note that it runs fiber networks and cell towers, which need energy. It also runs data centers, which are typically power-hungry. These three infrastructures heavily depend on electricity, which may become more expensive as oil prices spike.

Another thing to consider is the weakening currency against USD. The USD/PHP exchange rate has already broken above PHP 60 again after a long time. For many years, the exchange rate stayed between PHP 45-50 per dollar. But since 2022, it climbed to $50-56 before climbing again to $57-58. With that, PLDT may face higher import costs because it relies on foreign supplies for fiber optic cables, cell tower equipment, 5G infrastructure, and routers.

Mitigants

Amid all these things, I believe that PLDT is still a defensive stock in my portfolio.

One aspect to consider is that PLDT provides essential services and solutions, making its demand inelastic. Mobile connectivity, home broadband, and enterprise internet are crucial in our daily lives and day-to-day business operations. So, businesses and households won’t just cancel or cut their internet subscriptions.

Another thing is that telco businesses are not as cyclical as many other businesses like retail, property, banks, and airlines. Even in economic downturns, PLDT may even find opportunities to increase its mobile connectivity and home broadband penetration since there may be more time online as people try to keep up with current events.

In addition, its business model is recurring and structural and not cyclical. It earns mainly from monthly internet and telephone subscriptions, which means predictable cash flow and low dependence on one-time purchases. Its data demand may also rise amid the prevalence of hybrid work setups in the country.

Meanwhile, its data center operations may still boom in the long run. From a telecom company, PLDT expanded into digital infrastructure with its data center arm ePLDT and Vitro, which reported combined revenue growth of 22% YoY in 2025. I believe that it will take a long time to materialize considering the economic trajectory of PH at this point. But PH will have to adapt to the AI revolution to keep up with the global trends. This may require more internet, and data center capacity demand may increase as well. PLDT is also taking advantage of it by listing its data centers either as a REIT or as a monetizing business segment. With that, I believe PLDT could unlock billions in value, increase funding, and even lower its debt.

Its digital banking niche can also unlock more growth potential. As a digital banking user, Maya has become more and more popular each year. Initially an e-wallet, it has already transformed into a digital bank with higher interest rates to attract more users and depositors. This helps it increase its market coverage. Additionally, this move helps it reduce its exposure to competition with Gcash, which is a much more popular e-wallet here.

Lastly, PLDT maintains robust fundamentals despite being capital intensive. Its cash and cash equivalents remain adequate and stable at PHP 11.9B. It is also higher than in 2024, which means that its operations generated positive cash inflows in 2025. PLDT complements it with prudent debt management. As you can see, over 90% of the total borrowings will not mature within a year. More importantly, most of it has a maturity of five years and above. This is still in line with its Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.56x, which means that it only needs 2.56 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. This proves that it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operations and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model or DDM. Currently, its annualized dividend is $1.65, which is lower than in my previous coverage of $1.66. So, my dividend growth rate using five-year CAGR is 1.26%. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 6.0% to price in more risks since it is higher than the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, my estimated Beta is 0.52, which means that PLDT became less volatile than the S&P 500 and in my previous coverage. Using all this information, I derived a TP or target price of $26.72, which means that PLDT is still cheap at its current price. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $24.05.

I also updated its price ratios using book value for P/B, EPS for P/E, and sales to estimate P/S. As you can see, all three price ratios show that PLDT trades lower than the average in previous years. Their TPs range from $25 to $27, which is in line with my DDM TP. It is also worth noting that it only trades at a P/E multiple of 9.52x, which can be considered very cheap today. The other two ratios show that it trades at its cheapest level since 2021. With that, we can prove that the current price level is ideal for entry and may have already priced in enough risks.

Technicals

PLDT’s technicals are still weak as selling pressures persist. The stock price is already below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has already flattened, which suggests that recent market sentiments have already flattened from the bullish outlook.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the trend but indicate new buying opportunities. The MACD line is still decreasing below the signal line within negative territory along with the histogram. This shows a surge in selling volume, which has already outpaced buying volume. However, the increasing histogram close to zero implies that some buyers are taking the downtrend as an opportunity to enter. Its RSI supports this because it's already oversold.

Conclusion

PLDT, Inc. experiences slowing growth amid macroeconomic headwinds and increasing competition. But its defensive nature, supported by its robust fundamentals and strategic expansion, allows it to maintain its solid market and financial positioning. Its valuation is also still attractive and appears to have priced in enough risks. Technicals adhere to it, as overselling may lead to new buying opportunities. I'm upgrading my rating from hold to buy.

data.title: QTUM: The Oldest Quantum Computing ETF And Its Newest Rival
data.description:
Quantum computing has been hyped to the gills, but the nascent platform does have the potential to revolutionize industries. By dropping bits to leverage qubits via “entanglement” and “superposition," quantum could offer up solution sets well beyond the reach of traditional methods. It could radically advance biotech and the material sciences, with a projected $2 trillion economic impact by 2035.

Of course, it might not be all benign. Quantum may spawn a cybernetic chaos as the traditional cryptography that girds our military security, financial systems, and personal privacy is blown sky high, like chaff to the wind. The head-long corporate rush to quantum is also a sprint to head off quantum espionage, its evil twin.

So the stakes are high. Despite the incipience of the tech, many “pure play” quantum companies have gone public over the years, and the ETF industry has now embraced the theme as relevant. Launched in September 2018, Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM) is the most established player, with an impressive $3.45 billion under management. In contrast, WisdomTree Quantum Computing Fund ETF (WQTM) is its latest rival, first trading on October 9th, 2025.

Both funds offer interesting, if different investment strategies for the sector. However, QTUM is the better ETF to cope with today's deteriorating market conditions. Its expansive approach gives it more discretion to invest in many more facets of the quantum tech ecosystem.

The Two ETF and Their Holdings

QTUM is an ETF that seeks broad exposure to companies engaged in quantum computing, machine learning, and related technologies.

A passive fund, it tracks the BlueStar Quantum Computing & Machine Learning Index, which institutes an “equal-weighted” portfolio of global stocks. The fund is not cheap with an expense ratio of .40%, but it has demonstrated strong performance and offers an investor informed, diversified exposure to these still volatile segments of tech. It had a five star Morningstar rating for its 3 and 5 year time frames (ending 12/31/2025).

The BueStar index adjusts its holdings semi-annually (June, December), screening its investment universe for companies with business in: quantum computing and its application; advanced computing hardware (including GPUs, FPGAs, ASICs, etc); semiconductor / superconducting materials; equipment that helps in the creation of said computers; as well as big data and advanced sensor tech.

There are certain restrictions in size and liquidity –market caps must exceed $150 million for new inclusions with at least $21 million in 3-month average trading volume—though there are exceptions for IPOs and SPACs. The fund presently has 84 holdings, with a very equal-weighted disposition:

As you can see from the table, Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), QTUM’s largest holding, is only 2.08% of its total portfolio, with most tickers in the 1.50%-1.81% range. This approach certainly reduces volatility, particularly with large players in varying sectors, but it does not feel as targeted as the WisdomTree product (as you will see). Admittedly there is QUTM’s dual mandate of quantum and machine learning, but defense contractors Lockheed and Northrop feel outsized in the top ten while Quantum eMotion Corp. (QNC) stands alone as quantum "pure play."

In contrast, WQTM’s top holdings lean into very pure quantum stocks like Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI), IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS), and Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT) and it does so with large percentages. This gives you a lot more volatility but also far more focus on the core theme. The top 10 holdings make up 40.8% of the total, though they convey a healthy industry breakdown and a 33.05% non-US exposure. Strangely, EU-based Nokia Oyj (NOK) is the only stock the two ETFs share in the top ten.

Because it is new and sports a slightly higher expense ratio (.45%), WQTM has a tiny $25.97 million under management. Like QUTM, it is also a passive ETF, operating off an index called the WisdomTree Classiq Quantum Computing Index, something developed in conjunction with private company Classiq, a genuine leader in quantum algorithmic design.

Holdings in the fund are “capped” at a whopping 15% of total portfolio(!). They must have a market cap of at least $200 million and an average three-month trading volume of $1 million. They can be from DM or EM public markets. The fund is adjusted quarterly and filtered via a relevancy (“Pure Players” or “Diversified Innovators”) metric. It presently contains 42 holdings—exactly half of QTUM’s.

Performance

Looking at the performance of both ETFs suggests that QTUM’s broad, equal-weight portfolio has given it a great deal of ballast over the last six months. QTUM has lost a mere 0.46%. In comparison, WQTM has dropped a sizable 14.26%:

This flat calm does not mean that the Defiance ETF is a slouch. Since its launch in September 2018, the ticker is up a remarkable 349%(!), lapping the S&P 500’s 129%:

Likewise, the past six-month performance does not suggest that WQTM is a mutt. The ETF launched just before the October-November meltdown, has no dual, machine learning mandate, and is simply more levered to the rise and fall of the “Big Four of Quantum-ness,” which is what most investors might have to expect if pure exposure is what they are truly gunning for in a theme ETF.

Short-term, the quantum sector is a high volatility, “feast or famine” trade. Long-term, it contours into a very profitable, turbo-charged platform --one that picks the corporate winners and dispenses a certain “Schumpeterian creative destruction” to every facet of industry and scientific endeavor.

Risks

The risks of investing in quantum are myriad. Looking at QTUM’s long term chart (above) suggests just how volatile sector can be –it didn’t get back to its early 2022 price high until 2024.

Quantum still has its naysayers; it is still at an early stage. There has been progress, however: as I suggested in my report on "neutral atom" play Infleqtion, Inc. (INFQ), there are growing use cases for quantum clocks, GPS-agnostic navigation systems, and cryptography. Also fruitful: the emerging “separate hardware” approach, using GPUs for brute linear algebra of training and the QPUs (quantum processing units) for exponential inference speedups. It is affecting things: there is speculation that people fled bitcoin last fall not just because of the Genius Act or Gold 1.0, but because of quantum’s “just round da corner” capacity to break passwords.

QTUM has risks related to its tech concentration and, more specifically, the overbought semiconductor industry. It has the obvious currency exchange risk that might come from its 39% overseas holdings. For WQTM, its risks are even more pronounced, as it is very committed to several quantum computing companies jockeying in competition with platforms (trapped ion, photonic, neutral atom) that won’t be fully validated until that magical (but still distant) 100 logical qubit threshold. Like the automobile wars of the 1900-1920 period, some of these companies might not survive the shake-out.

Conclusion

Investors confident that qubit breakthroughs and client buy-ins can keep the sector levitating, even through a weakening economy and a market drawdown, might find WQTM’s “swing for the fences approach” worth the bet.

However, the broader market conditions can't be denied. Private credit woes, high oil prices, and a tapped out US consumer suggests 2026 will be a period of multiple compression. Due to our less sanguine outlook, QTUM’s expansive approach is the better bet. It should allow the ETF to better endure a lackluster market, to opportunistically lean into other parts of the supply chain and appreciate even when the pure play theme is out of favor.

data.title: Smithfield Foods: Nathan's Buyout In Focus Ahead Of Q4
data.description:
Shares in packaged meats and fresh pork food company Smithfield Foods (SFD) are outperforming broader markets, and I don’t believe the positive momentum will be derailed anytime soon.

The stock is up nearly 20% over the past year, including a 5.5% increase YTD. This compares to losses held by the broader S&P 500 (SPY). SFD will be reporting its Q4 on March 24, and ahead of that release, I believe the stock is fairly valued.

Investors will likely be honing in on the deal to acquire Nathan’s Famous (NATH) as well as on SFD’s facility expansion plans. Of course, commodity pricing and SFD’s exposure to current world affairs will also likely be a topic of discussion within the earnings call.

While I believe upside potential is there for SFD, I prefer a "Hold" position, with the belief that investors could be better suited waiting for a more attractive entry point.

SFD Stock Key Metrics

Shares in SFD are viewed favorably across the board. At current trading levels, the stock carries a forward multiple of just 10x earnings. That’s a significant discount to the broader market indexes, which currently fetch a mid-20x multiple on an overall basis.

In addition, SFD’s current enterprise value is just 0.7x its forward sales. This is well below the sector average of about 1.4x. It’s not a surprise, then, that SFD receives a positive mark from Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quant scores.

Likewise, both the SA analyst community and Wall Street are bullish on SFD’s prospects. In fairness, SFD is lightly covered among the SA community, so the bullish sentiment is among a rather small pool. The same can be said of Wall Street, which is seeing just seven analysts cover the stock over the past 90 days.

That said, consensus price targets still see significant upside remaining in shares. With an average target of $30/share, this would imply nearly 30% upside from current trading levels.

What Is The Outlook For SFD Stock?

I view SFD’s overall forward outlook as positive.

The management team is continuing to guide for low- to mid-single-digit revenue growth alongside solid profitability expansion, driven primarily by the strength of its Packaged Meats segment.

This segment really is the core engine of the company, consistently generating over +$1 billion in operating profit and benefiting from favorable long-term trends, including steady protein demand, consumer preference for convenience, and product innovation. Given the current economic environment, it’s partially surprising how resilient the model has been.

Even in a challenging environment marked by elevated raw material costs and cautious consumer spending, SFD has continued to post record operating profits and even raise guidance. In my view, that speaks to the strength of its continued pricing power.

There are, of course, some offsets to consider. Industry-wide volume softness and ongoing commodity volatility, for example, and particularly in hog and feed costs, which remain key swing factors. That said, SFD has done a solid job mitigating these pressures through cost controls and operational efficiencies.

What to Watch When SFD Reports Q4 Results?

Heading into the Q4 print, I believe the biggest focal point will almost certainly be the pending NATH acquisition and how management frames its strategic impact.

While the deal itself won’t close until later this year, investors should expect meaningful commentary around integration plans, expected synergies, and the broader role NATH will play within SFD's Packaged Meats segment.

From my perspective, this deal is more than just a bolt-on acquisition. It effectively converts a long-standing licensing relationship into full ownership, giving SFD complete control over a highly recognizable brand.

The management team has already pointed to roughly +$9 million in annual cost synergies and immediate EPS accretion, so any additional color around timing or upside potential will be key.

Beyond M&A, I’ll also be watching how trends outlined in Q3 have progressed. In particular, updates on raw material inflation, consumer demand elasticity, and overall pricing power.

Packaged Meats margins have held up well, but investors will want reassurance that this strength is sustainable in the current market environment.

Lastly, any commentary on capital allocation, especially tied to the new South Dakota facility, could draw attention.

All considered, this should be a fairly eventful earnings call with several moving pieces worth monitoring.

Is SFD Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At current levels, I view shares in SFD as a "Hold."

On one hand, the fundamentals remain solid, and the Packaged Meats segment continues to deliver consistent profitability even in a tougher macro environment. Add in the NATH acquisition, which should enhance long-term growth and margin stability, and the broader story remains positive.

Valuation also isn’t stretched. Trading at around 10x forward earnings and below peer averages on a sales basis, SFD still comes off as relatively inexpensive to me. That helps support the bull case, particularly with consensus estimates implying meaningful upside from here.

That said, I don’t think this is the most compelling entry point in the near term. The stock has already had a solid run, and with the Q4 release right around the corner, there’s potential for volatility depending on how management frames near-term pressures like input costs and consumer demand trends.

Additionally, while the NATH deal is strategically sound through my lens, execution and integration will take time, and the benefits likely won’t be fully realized immediately.

Overall, I think SFD is a name worth owning, but I’d prefer to wait for either a pullback or additional clarity post-earnings before getting more aggressive on the long side.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Rate Cut Hopes Crumble
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

BoE hawkish turn wipes out market hopes for Fed cuts. (0:16) Alibaba posts wide quarterly misses as AI spending pressures profits. (1:47) Oscars audience falls, while global music revenue keeps rising. (2:46)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Out top story so far, to paraphrase Kaiser Soze: just like that, it’s gone.

Markets now see little chance the Federal Reserve cuts rate this year. Just a month ago, about 50 basis points of easing were priced in.

Fed funds futures now imply nearly an 80% chance that rates will end 2026 where they are today — and a 5% probability they could even be higher — as global central banks picked up the hawkish baton from Wednesday’s FOMC meeting and ran with it.

“Take one hawkish-leaning FOMC presser, mix in a hawkish hold from the BoJ, sprinkle on top a BoE 9-0 hold vote — and what do you get? A mess,” said Guy LeBas, strategist at Janney.

The Bank of England especially rattled the cage, signaling potential rate hikes to combat energy spikes. Not long ago, two rate cuts were expected this year. Now roughly 60 basis points of hikes are priced in.

Near-term gilt yields surged to 52-week highs. In the U.S., the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) topped 3.85%, levels not seen since July.

Michael Brown of Pepperstone warned the BoE’s tone “implies that the MPC may be on the verge of making a horrific policy mistake.”

He argued the economy is in a very different place than during the last energy shock, with a more fragile labor market and lower risk of persistent price pressures.

Markets found some relief when the ECB, which also held rates, sounded less alarmed — though it left the door open to a potential April hike.

The ECB “obviously looked at the BoE statement today and thought ‘OK, let’s not do anything THAT crazy,” economist Dario Perkins said.

Among active stocks, Alibaba (BABA) reported wide fourth-quarter misses on both revenue and profit, raising fresh questions about whether its heavy AI investments are paying off.

Seeking Alpha analyst Geneva Investor noted that “AI investments come at the detriment of net income,” which fell 67% year-over-year. The analyst added that cloud revenue growth of 36% year-over-year may not be enough when Microsoft’s Azure is expanding at 39% from a much larger base.

Five Below (FIVE) is rallying after fourth-quarter sales jumped 24.5% from a year ago. Comparable sales rose 15.3%, easily topping the 13.7% consensus estimate.

And Alcoa (AA) is tumbling after aluminum futures on the London Metal Exchange posted their biggest drop since 2018, erasing a portion of the recent war premium.

Analysts at ANZ said that while buyers across Asia, Europe and the U.S. are scrambling to secure supply, stockpiles in China continue to build — tempering some of the bullish price pressure.

In other news of notes, let’s lighten things up a little with some entertainment headline.

The 98th Academy Awards drew 17.86M viewers across ABC and Hulu (DIS), the lowest total since 2022. Nielsen data showed viewership fell more than 9% from last year, snapping a multi-year recovery. After hitting an all-time low of 10.4M in 2021 during the pandemic, the Oscars rebounded for three straight years, peaking at 19.7M in 2025.

Digital engagement, however, remained strong. ABC reported a 42% jump in social impressions to more than 184M.

And in more encouraging news for the industry, global recorded music revenue rose 6.4% to $31.7B in 2025, driven by growth in paid subscriptions.

The nostalgically named International Federation of the Phonographic Industry said revenue from paid streaming platforms climbed 8.8% and now accounts for more than half of global industry revenue.

Latin America led regional growth, up 17% from 2024.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, we turn to neglected stocks. BofA Securities has tallied the names where hedge funds are most broadly underweight in each sector.

They include Super Micro Computer (SMCI) in Info Tech; Realty Income (O) in Real Estate; Hasbro (HAS) in Consumer Discretionary; and Kinder Morgan (KMI) in Energy.

Check out the full list here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Crude Oil Price Forecast: Analyzing The Bullish $150 Case And Bearish $95 Threat
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Oil prices surged in early trade today as Brent crude oil prices surged toward a high near the $114 per barrel mark.

The rally, which saw Brent (CO1:COM) hit a high of $114 and WTI (CL1:COM) cross the $105 mark, was triggered by a dangerous escalation in Middle Eastern hostilities that now directly threatens the world’s most vital energy infrastructure.

The Catalyst: Attacks on Energy Hearts

The primary driver behind the price spike was a series of kinetic strikes on Iranian oil facilities and the subsequent expansion of the conflict. Reports confirmed Israeli airstrikes targeting Iran's South Pars gas field, but the US and Qatar were not involved, Trump said late Wednesday.

In a retaliatory move that sent shockwaves through trading floors, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) issued a formal warning that it would target oil installations in Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), and Qatar.

Because these three nations represent approximately 20-25% of global crude exports, the threat transformed a regional conflict into a systemic risk for the global economy.

Recent missile and drone attacks have caused major damage to energy facilities across several countries. Some of the attacks are listed below.

Qatar: Missiles hit the world’s largest LNG (natural gas) plants and a major Shell facility, stopping production and causing European gas prices to spike.

Saudi Arabia: The military stopped several missiles and drones, but an aerial attack on a refinery in Yanbu briefly disrupted oil shipments.

Kuwait: A drone strike started a fire at a refinery, though it was contained.

For context, Iranian attacks have knocked out 17% of Qatar's liquefied natural gas (LNG) export capacity, causing an estimated $20 billion in lost annual revenue and threatening supplies to Europe and Asia, QatarEnergy's CEO told Reuters on Thursday.

QatarEnergy had declared force majeure on its entire output of LNG, after earlier attacks on its Ras Laffan production hub, which came under fire again on Wednesday.

In response to the rising tension, the Trump administration is reportedly considering sending thousands more US troops to the region to support ongoing operations.

The "Hormuz factor" and regional divergence

At the center of the market's anxiety is the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 21 million barrels of oil and refined products pass through this narrow waterway daily. With Iran threatening a full blockade and shipping lanes becoming increasingly unsafe, a massive "risk premium" has been baked into every barrel.

Think of it as a "regional divergence" - at least that is how the shock is being felt. Asian markets, including China, India, and Japan, are at the epicenter of the crisis because they rely most heavily on Gulf barrels.

While Western benchmarks like Brent and WTI rose sharply, the physical shortage is currently most acute in Asia due to shipping times. It takes roughly 10-15 days for Gulf oil to reach Asia, whereas rerouted cargoes heading to Europe or the US via the Cape of Good Hope can take up to 45 days.

This "lagged transmission" suggests that while prices are already high, the full impact of the supply disruption has yet to reach the Atlantic basin, and thus, prices could potentially surge even more if the conflict continues to drag on.

Economic Fallout: From Pumps to Portfolios

The surge to $109 is already manifesting in "creeping price increases" for consumer goods. Transport operators and airlines have already warned of imminent fare hikes, while manufacturers brace for a "margin squeeze."

If prices remain at these levels, the global economy faces a renewed inflationary wave that could force central banks to keep interest rates higher for longer, stifling growth just as many regions were eyeing a recovery.

Outlook: Where to Next?

The trajectory of oil prices now hinges on two critical variables: the duration of the disruption and the risk of further escalation.

The Bullish Case ($120-150): Technical analysis suggests that if Brent closes decisively above the $113.75 resistance level, the market could rapidly retest the 2022 peaks of $130, with a move toward $150 possible if the Strait of Hormuz is fully closed or if Saudi facilities sustain major damage.

The Bearish Case (Below $95): Conversely, if diplomatic efforts which have so far failed suddenly gain traction, or if the US and its allies announce a massive coordinated release from Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR), prices could retreat. A fall below the $97.65 support level would be required to signal that the current "geopolitical fever" is breaking.

Another area to keep an eye on is the US, where in order to help lower global energy costs and increase the available supply, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced on Thursday that the government might lift sanctions on Iranian oil currently stuck on tankers.

By releasing this "stranded" oil into the market, the administration hopes to provide much-needed relief to rising global prices.

For now, the market remains in a state of "physical tightness." With inventory buffers in the West beginning to draw down and no end in sight to the regional war, the path of least resistance for oil prices appears to be higher. Investors and consumers alike should prepare for a volatile second quarter as the world navigates one of the most significant energy disruptions in recent history.

The Brent Crude H1 chart shows a sharp rejection from the $114.00 level, following today's spike. The price has broken below the 20-period SMA (green line), signaling a loss of immediate upward momentum.

Key Technical Observations

Resistance: The $114.00-115.00 zone remains a major ceiling. A daily close above this level could open up a run toward the $120 mark and beyond.

Support: Immediate support sits at $104.22 (50-day MA), which also just rests above the key swing high from Monday, March 16 at 103.51, followed by the significant psychological level of $102.14 (100-day MA) before the psychological 100.00 mark comes back into play.

RSI: The Relative Strength Index has dropped sharply from overbought territory to 49.37, suggesting the market is cooling off and entering a neutral phase where momentum may be shifting in favor of bears.

Outlook

The short-term trend is turning cautiously bearish. If the price fails to hold above $106.00, we could see a deeper retracement toward the $104.22 support level. However, given the geopolitical risks, any further supply shocks could quickly invalidate this technical pullback and push prices back toward $120.00.

Original Post

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: Phase 3 Obesity Data Feels Like A Coin Toss (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last updated on Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (VKTX) in October last year, covering the company's Q3 earnings and business updates, and issued stock with a Buy rating - my sixth straight Buy call, in fact, following notes in September 2024, December 2024, April 2025, July 2025, and August 2025.

Since December 2024, VKTX stock is in fact down >45%, but up nearly 30% since my April 2025 note, and flat since my December update.

I have been following Viking closely because my thesis around the company has been quite consistent. Viking's lead candidate today is VK2735, which is a dual agonist of the glucagon-like peptide 1, or GLP-1, and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide, i.e., a member of the GLP-1 drug class.

The best known members of this drug class are Eli Lilly's (LLY) tirzepatide, also a GLP/GIP dual agonist, approved to treat obesity and Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"), brand names Zepbound / Mounjaro, and Novo Nordisk's (NVO) semaglutide, which targets GLP-1 only, approved in the same indications as Wegovy / Mounjaro. These drugs' combined sales in 2025 exceeded $70bn.

These two drug molecules / four products are still the latest GLP-1 drugs to be approved by the FDA (Zepbound was the last to be approved, in November 2023), despite the best efforts of a range of biotech and pharma companies to develop and win approval for similar drugs, and to grab a slice of what many analysts believe will be the biggest ever pharmaceutical market - treating obese patients or patients with Type 2 diabetes with GLP-1 class drugs.

Companies to be aware of in this space include Pharma giants Roche (RHHBY), Amgen (AMGN), AstraZeneca (AZN), AbbVie (ABBV), Pfizer (PFE), Regeneron (REGN) and Merck (MRK), and biotechs Altimmune (ALT), Structure Therapeutics Inc (GPCR), MBX Biosciences Inc (MBX), Septerna Inc (SEPN), and Zealand Pharma, amongst others.

Very few of these companies have advanced their candidates as far as Viking has, however. Viking has two Phase 3 studies of VK2735 ongoing, VANQUISH-1, study of ~4,500 patients with obesity, and VANQUISH-2, a study of ~1,100 patients with obesity and Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"). Both studies are placebo controlled, use a maximum dose of 17.5mg, and last for 78 weeks.

A phase 3 study of an oral version of VK2735 is planned to initiate later this year, and a Phase 1 study maintenance study that sees patients switch from from a subcutaneous injection to an oral pill is also ongoing.

VK2735 presents some intriguing potential advantages over rival GLP-1 drugs, for example, an extended half-life, which may mean it can be dosed less frequently than tirzepatide or semaglutide, which are once-weekly injectables, the ability to offer it orally and as an injectable therapy (admittedly, oral Wegovy is already on the market, and Lilly's oral GLP-1 orforglipron will likely soon join it), and a competitive safety profile.

In summary, then, Viking seems to have a drug candidate whose early data readouts suggest an approval submission following a positive Phase study readout could be successful, meaning Viking, which reported ~$700m cash as of the end of Q4 2025, could potentially become the third player in a >$70bn revenue per annum that analysts believe could be worth double that figure by 2030.

Given Viking's market cap valuation today is just $3.9bn (shares are trading at $34 at the time of writing), while we must acknowledge the risk that a study setback or increased competition could impact Viking's earning potential, the bull case is made by the fact Viking may have an asset capable of generating multibillion dollars of revenues per annum.

In that scenario, based on the valuations of pharma companies generating similar revenues, the company may merit a double-digit billion valuation, implying there is >100% potential upside in play, but inevitably, there are risks and caveats to that thesis, as I will explain in this note.

Let's begin this latest update with an overview of Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings, and business updates.

Viking Post 2025 Earnings - Setting Itself Up For Sustained Success

Viking released its earnings on February 11th - the company reported no revenues, spent $345m on research and development ("R&D"), had general and administrative costs of $48.3m and made a loss from operations of $(393.3m), or $(3.19) per share.

Viking reported cash and equivalents of $165.8m, and short term investments of $540m. As I wrote in my last note:

This is thanks largely to a $633m fundraising completed in February 2024, at $85 per share, capitalizing on a surge in its stock price after it reported Phase 2 study results for injectable VK2735 showing 13.1% placebo adjusted weight loss in patients after 13 weeks of treatment.

Viking also shared some intriguing business updates. For example, that the VANQUISH-1 study was now fully enrolled, and that VANQUISH-2 was close to full enrollment. The Phase 1 maintenance study was also reported to be fully enrolled.

On its earnings call, CEO Brian Lian also reported that Viking had:

signed a comprehensive manufacturing and supply agreement with CordenPharma to support the potential commercialization of VK2735.

Under the terms of the agreement, CordenPharma will provide large-scale supply of active pharmaceutical ingredients, as well as fill and finish capacities for both our subcutaneous and oral formulations.

We believe this agreement provides supply potentially sufficient to support a multibillion-dollar revenue opportunity.

Viking also appointed Neil Aubuchon as the company's Chief Commercial Officer - Aubuchon joined from the drug discovery biotech AbCellera, but also had stints at Pharma giants Amgen (AMGN) and Eli Lilly, where he spent 17 years - seemingly a good fit for a company in the GLP-1 drug development space.

In summary, then, operationally speaking, the company appears to be in a good place, with two Phase 3 studies nearly fully enrolled, a Phase 3 oral study expected to start this year, a manufacturing partner, new commercial lead, and sufficient cash to see the company through the Phase 3 studies, fund a regulatory submission, and initiate a commercial launch, if approved.

The Potential Pitfall For Investors To Consider - Data To Date Is From Small Patient Populations

I mentioned the surge in Viking's share price in February 2024, which has ultimately proven short-lived - shares are down 60% since that high was reached, and it was based on a small sample of patients.

As we can see above (source: Viking corporate presentation) the 14.7% reduction in weight (or 13.1%, placebo adjusted) was recorded in the highest dose cohort, involving just 35 patients and as we can see below, in that cohort, some 29% of patients experienced mild nausea, 20% diarrhea, and 26% constipation.

My guess would be that if Viking shared the same data set today, the market would possibly not react with such enthusiasm, as there have been a good number of comparable or arguably better data readouts in larger patient data sets since.

To name one or two, Eli Lilly's pipeline candidate retatrutide delivered a mean weight loss percentage of 28.7% in a study of >450 patients with obesity and knee osteoarthritis, while Amgen's MariTide achieved ~20% average weight loss in people living with obesity without T2D, in a 52-week study, using a monthly dosing regime.

In terms of durability of response, ability to dose monthly, and safety profile, then, we might speculate that Viking still has it all to prove, although there are some other promising readouts to share since 2024.

There is the oral study data, for example - a Phase 1 study 8.2% weight loss in patients receiving the highest (100mg) dose, or 6.8% placebo adjusted at just 28 days, with "effects largely maintained" after 57 days, and 4 weeks after the last study dose. The tolerability profile of the drug was also very strong, a 67% rate of mild nausea the only negative to note.

The Venture study achieved 12% reduction from baseline with no plateau, with the "majority of weight loss maintained for an additional 3 weeks following completion of dosing," while an exploratory cohort suggested a lower maintenance dose "may further extend body weight reduction."

In terms of data to come, as mentioned in my last note, I am not sure we will see any Phase 3 data until 2027, but there is an intriguing catalysts to note upcoming in 2026. Viking initiated its Phase 1 maintenance dose study in October last year, which will enroll ~180 patients. As per Viking's earnings release:

All participants will receive initial weekly subcutaneous doses of VK2735 or placebo for 19 weeks. Following Week 19, participants are transitioned to a range of VK2735 maintenance dosing regimens including weekly, every other week, and monthly subcutaneous dosing, as well as daily oral dosing, weekly oral dosing, or placebo.

In January 2026, Viking announced completion of enrollment in the maintenance dosing study. The company expects to report the results of the study in 3Q26.

Positive results from this study will show that Viking may have the most flexible GLP-1 drug, in terms of patients being able to switch from injectable to pill (or presumably, vice versa), and dose more or less frequently, without having to switch from one compound to another, as, for example patients using injectable tirzepatide might have to do when switching to oral orforglipron, a different compound. In the case of Novo Nordisk, patients can presumably switch from injectable Wegovy to oral Wegovy, although Wegovy targets GLP-1 only, not GIP / GLP.

Concluding Thoughts"- Is The "Double Your Money On a Positive Phase 3 Readout" Still On?

This is the tricky part of the Viking investment equation. I would conclude that yes, if the Phase 3 studies of VK2735 succeed, Viking's stock will surge in value, perhaps testing former highs of $85, and allowing Viking to complete another mega-funding round.

On the other hand, are there compelling reasons to believe the study will be a resounding success? It is arguably hard to say Viking's study is more derisked than, say, Amgen's Phase 3 studies of MariTide, given Amgen completed a successful Phase 2 study in >500 patients.

Furthermore, Pfizer (PFE) apparently expects to be running ten Phase 3 studies of GLP-1 drugs by the end of 2026, including its "ultra-long-acting" GLP-1 RA PF3944, a drug it acquired via its buyout of Metsera last year, in a deal which could rise to $10bn.

Frankly, all of this progress being made by rival GLP-1 drug developers concerns me, as it piles the pressure on Viking, while the leap from testing a handful of patients, to testing >5k patients, is so large, how certain can we be the outcome will be positive?

In my view, Viking does run a serious risk of being an "also-ran" in the race for the next marketable GLP-1 drug, as it ultimately seems to have been in the race to win approval for a metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (“MASH”).

Its candidate VK2809 was challenging Madrigal Pharmaceuticals resmetirom in that indication, but while Madrigal secured approval for resmetirom (brand name Rezdiffra) and earned nearly $1bn of revenues in 2025, the VK2809 program appears to be in abeyance.

Some commenters responding to my previous posts have laid the blame for Viking's slow progress at the door of management, for being slow to execute, but we can see for almost certain that we will have our answer on whether VK2735 is approvable or not early in 2027, when we see the Phase 3 results.

The equation is again simple - succeed, and Viking's stock price could rise to double what it is today. Fail, and it will likely halve in value overnight. I, therefore, conclude that calling Buy or Sell on Viking stock at the present time almost feels like the toss of a coin, and that's why I feel I have no option but to downgrade my Buy rating to a Hold rating at this time.

If Viking were the only company with a Phase 3 stage GLP-1 asset in development outside of Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, the gamble on a positive Phase 3 would arguably be worth taking. Today, I wonder if the competition is becoming too intense. My best advice would be to wait and see what the Phase 1 maintenance data, due Q3, looks like, and then re-evaluate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alibaba: Mixed Quarter, But I'm Staying The Course
data.description:
Analyzing Alibaba's Q3 FY2026 Report

For Q3 FY2026, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) missed top and bottom line estimates, with revenues of $40.73B [+2% y/y] falling short of the consensus estimate of $41.28B - primarily due to weaker-than-expected performance in its domestic (China) and international e-commerce businesses.

Furthermore, on the earnings front, Alibaba reported a significant miss on non-GAAP EPS that landed at $1.01 per share [-67% y/y] versus the consensus expectation of $1.59 per share.

However, as we have discussed in the past, Alibaba's aggressive investment cycle in Quick Commerce and AI + Cloud is temporarily compressing profitability here. And, given robust growth in both segments [Quick Commerce revenue +56% y/y, Cloud revenue +36% y/y], I think prudent investors have enough impetus to look past the earnings shortfall.

Also, excluding the impact of SunArt and Intime divestments, Alibaba's quarterly revenue was up by a respectable ~9% y/y [albeit, a notable deceleration from ~15% y/y growth in Q2 FY2026]:

Now, the slowdown in Alibaba's consumption business is certainly alarming [weaker transaction activity could be an indication of broader consumption weakness (macro) and/or increased competitive pressures], but the growth in Quick Commerce remains strong enough to justify management's ongoing investments in this area, plus improved unit economics are already creating less of a negative impact on adj. EBITA for the China E-commerce Group.

The Cloud business is already quite profitable on an adj. EBITA basis, with an ever-improving margin profile. And, within Cloud Intelligence Group [$6.2B, +36% y/y], AI-related revenues are still growing at a triple-digit y/y clip. Over the long run, Alibaba's Cloud Intelligence Group could turn into its primary profit center, much like AWS has been for Amazon (AMZN).

Now, with Alibaba's consumption business margins currently suppressed and non-core businesses still losing money, Alibaba's consolidated adj. EBITA landed at $3.35B (down 57% y/y) in Q3 FY2026.

On a positive note, Alibaba's quarterly free cash flow flipped back into positive territory, with the Chinese tech conglomerate producing +$1.6B in FCF during Q3 FY2026. Now, considering its ongoing investment cycle, I expect ongoing cash flow wobbles to continue for the foreseeable future; however, Alibaba's robust balance sheet [net cash of $42B+] gives me the comfort to stay invested through this spending cycle.

Concluding Thoughts: Is BABA Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my previous report, I shared the following -

Looking forward, I continue to see two clear strategic pillars of growth for Alibaba: consumption and AI + Cloud. Now, while Alibaba's cash flow wobbles are likely to persist for the foreseeable future due to its humongous investment cycle, over the long term, I believe Alibaba's ongoing investments can yield significant returns for the company's shareholders.

While Alibaba clearly failed to live up to the pre-earnings report expectations, I am content with the momentum in Quick Commerce and Cloud businesses.

Based on reasonable assumptions for future sales growth and optimized FCF margins, TQI's fair value estimate for BABA stock is ~$219 per share in light of the Q3 FY2026 earnings report [down from ~$223 post-Q2].

With Alibaba stock currently trading in the $120s, I deem it significantly undervalued and see a near-term upside of +75% from current levels.

Note: For today's exercise, all assumptions from our previous assessment were held, barring the model's revenue base, which has been reduced to FY2026E sales of $145B.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Now, despite using a discounted exit multiple of 15x P/FCF for Alibaba, TQI's 5-year expected CAGR return for BABA stock is ~23.5%. And, since this figure exceeds our investment hurdle rate of 15%, Alibaba stock is a "Buy" under our valuation process.

From my previous update in November, Alibaba's stock has declined from the $150s to the $120s. While Alibaba's Q3 FY2026 report failed to excite, I see no reason to alter my bullish stance on the stock. The long-term business growth drivers are still exhibiting momentum, and the risk/reward of BABA stock is even more appealing now despite the slight reductions in our fair value estimates and 5-year price target.

Key Takeaway: I continue to rate Alibaba a Buy in the $120s, with preference for staggered accumulation.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing! Please share your thoughts, questions, and/or concerns in the comments section below.

data.title: Record Highs On The Horizon For The VEGI ETF
data.description:
In a March 10, 2026, Seeking Alpha article on fertilizers and The Mosaic Company (MOS), I explained that:

While the crude oil market is in the crosshairs of the war in Iran, other commodities that rely on the same logistical route are also affected. Fertilizers are in the eye of the current storm.

Meanwhile, a March 18, 2026, posting by SA New Editor Carl Surran noted:

Agricultural futures have yet to feel the full force of the Middle East war, Bank of America said in a note, as volatility unleashed in oil and natural gas prices already feeds into the cost of inputs such as fertilizer and fuel, but U.S. production for crops such as corn may be challenged if fertilizer remains more expensive and harder to get.

The iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF (VEGI) has been moving higher since April 2025, and the current environment could push it to a new all-time high.

War compounds the usual uncertainty heading into the Northern Hemisphere’s 2026 crop year

The annual planting season in the Northern Hemisphere begins in March and April when farmers plant the crops that feed, and increasingly fuel, the world. The planting season is typically a volatile time in agricultural markets, as weather conditions during the crop year ultimately determine whether producers’ output can satisfy the growing global demand.

In March 2026, another factor intensified uncertainty, as the war in Iran and blockages of the Strait of Hormuz has impacted the global fertilizer market. While the world is primarily focused on crude oil and natural gas supplies, the war’s impact on fertilizer supplies is timely, as shortages could affect annual global crop output. Unlike the oil market, there are no strategic fertilizer stockpiles, as most farmers, who are the consumers, purchase their fertilizer requirements on a hand-to-mouth basis, only exacerbating the potential for shortages.

Timing in markets is everything, and the war’s timing, which could create fertilizer shortages, could not be worse for 2026 crop production.

The iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF invests in agriculture-related companies

The fund profile for the iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF states:

VEGI’s top holdings include:

VEGI’s top holding is Deere & Co (DE), the leading farm equipment manufacturer. VEGI also has significant exposure to fertilizer, agricultural processing, and other related companies. A full list of the 183 holdings of the VEGI ETF is available on the iShares website.

At around $44.50 per share, VEGI had $121.59 million in assets under management. VEGI trades an average of over 80,000 shares per day and charges a 0.39% management fee. The annual $0.90 dividend yields 2%, covering the expense ratio in under one quarter.

A bullish trend since April 2025

VEGI dropped to a low in April 2025 when the U.S. administration shocked markets across all asset classes with its “Liberation Day” tariff announcement. Since then, VEGI has taken off on the upside.

The fifteen-year chart shows that VEGI declined to a $33.13 low in April 2025. Over the past year, VEGI has made higher lows and higher highs, reaching its most recent high of $47.27 in February 2026. At its most recent high, VEGI had posted a 42.7% gain since April 2025 and was trading around $44.50 on March 19, not far below its February 2026 peak.

The trend in VEGI shares is higher, with the next technical target at the April 2022 record high of $50.61 per share.

Positive Seeking Alpha ETF Grades

VEGI receives mostly positive Seeking Alpha ETF Grades in March 2026.

As the chart shows, given its rally since the April 2025 low, VEGI earns an A- grade in the momentum category. The 0.39% management fee earns a B- grade in the expenses category, and the 2% yield received a C in the dividend category. The assets under management and daily trading volume earn a C- grade in liquidity, while the risk of highly volatile agricultural commodity prices receives a D+ grade in the risk category.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating rates VEGI a Buy, after an upgrade on February 4, 2026, from its previous Hold rating. VEGI was upgraded from a Sell in mid-January 2026, and it was a Strong Sell in late 2025.

The reasons I rate VEGI a buy in March 2026

I rate VEGI a buy on March 19, 2026, for the following compelling reasons:

  • Rose to a record high at over $50 per share in April 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine. While the war in Ukraine continues, the war in Iran has disrupted a critical logistical chokepoint, potentially affecting 2026 Northern Hemisphere crop yields.
  • Fertilizer shortages could boost grain and oilseed prices over the coming weeks and months.
  • Grain and oilseed prices had been in bearish trends since their 2022 highs. Corn, wheat, and soybean futures prices made lows in 2025 and have turned higher as the 2026 crop year approaches.
  • Aside from the rising potential of fertilizer shortages, weather over the coming months always creates uncertainty during the planting season and could impact crop yields.
  • Commodity cyclicality supports a continued recovery in agricultural commodity prices.
  • Elevated inflationary pressures increase production costs, supporting agricultural commodity prices.
  • Global population growth increases the demand for agricultural products.
  • The rising demand for biofuels is bullish for agricultural commodity prices.
  • VEGI’s trend since the April 2025 low is bullish, and the trend is always a trader’s or investor’s best friend.

As the 2026 Northern Hemisphere crop season gets underway and the war in Iran threatens global fertilizer supplies, agricultural commodity prices could rise. I expect the VEGI to challenge the April 2022 $50.61 high and to continue making higher lows and higher highs over the coming weeks and months.
